name: logo-reveal-light-sweep
description: Logo reveal with CC Light Sweep sheen, ADBE Glow, and inertial bounce settle on scale. 1920x1080, 5s duration. Designed for brand idents, show opens, packaging stings, and any context requiring a polished logo entrance. The logo starts invisible at 90% scale, fades and scales in with easeOutCubic, then a light sweep animates across the logo surface followed by a soft glow. Uses inertial bounce expression on scale for a living, physical quality on arrival.

design_notes.palette: Dark background #0B0B0F (§3.1 near-black). Logo itself is assumed to carry its own colors. Glow color defaults to warm white #F5F5F5 which works over most logo colors.
design_notes.timing: Logo entrance: 0.8s (§1.2 'Simple logo reveal: 18–36 frames @24fps = 750ms–1.5s'). Light sweep: 0.8s across the logo surface (§2.4 CC Light Sweep technique). Hold: 5s total, entrance 0–0.8s, hold 0.8–5.0s. Per §1.5 pacing: 'Logo/brand mark hold: 48–96 frames = 2–4 seconds' — at 29.97fps hold of 4s = 120 frames, exceeds 96-frame max recommendation but appropriate for full 5s comp.
design_notes.easing: easeOutCubic (0.215, 0.61, 0.355, 1) for scale and opacity entrance — 'Cubic Family: most used by professionals' per §1.1. The inertial bounce expression on scale provides the overshoot-settle that communicates weight and life per §1.6.
design_notes.bounce: Inertial Bounce (Harry Frank / Gray Machine, §1.4): amp=0.03, freq=3.0, decay=5.0 — 'Spring UI element' preset. Single clean bounce that settles in ~0.4s. This makes the logo feel like it has mass rather than being a flat digital element.
design_notes.light_sweep: CC Light Sweep (§2.4): Direction animates 0° to 180° over 0.8s starting at t=0.8 (when logo is fully visible). Width 120, Intensity 80%, Shape Sharp for metallic sheen quality. Sweeps from left edge to right edge.
design_notes.glow: ADBE Glow applied after light sweep for sustained luminosity. Glow Radius 30, Glow Intensity 0.6, Glow Colors: original colors. Provides ambient presence without overexposing the logo.

logo_text.description: Placeholder text for the logo layer (replace with actual logo import in production). Used here as a shape/text layer proxy.

background_color.description: RGB 0–1 range. Default #0B0B0F near-black. The dark background maximizes logo contrast and light sweep visibility.

sweep_intensity.description: CC Light Sweep intensity (0–100). 80% gives a strong metallic sheen. Reduce to 40–60% for a subtler, premium quality reveal.

glow_intensity.description: ADBE Glow intensity (0–1). 0.6 creates visible but non-oversaturating glow. Reduce to 0.3 for subtle ambient, increase to 1.0 for neon-adjacent look.

bounce_amplitude.description: Inertial bounce expression amp parameter. 0.03 = Spring UI element (subtle). Increase to 0.1 for more playful bounce. See §1.4 parameter table.

steps.note: Background #0B0B0F = near-black with cool blue cast (§3.1). The dark background is essential for both the light sweep (needs dark field so the sweep stands out) and the glow (glow effects are additive and only visible against dark areas).

Dedicated background solid. Placing the BG as a layer (not just comp background) allows effects like a subtle gradient or particle system to be applied above it but below the logo. This is good production hygiene for flexible compositing.

Apply Gradient Ramp (ADBE Ramp) to BG solid for subtle background depth. A plain flat dark solid feels dead under a logo. A subtle radial or linear ramp from #0B0B0F to a slightly lighter value (#111827 = surface) creates perceived depth — the center appears slightly lifted, drawing focus to the logo area.

Gradient Shape = Radial (value 2). Per §3.4: 'Radial gradients create a natural focal point — the eye is drawn to the center/hotspot. Used to simulate light sources.' Radial gradient centered on the frame places the brightest point at the logo position.
Ramp Start Point at [960, 540] — frame center. The radial gradient emanates from the center where the logo lives.

Ramp Start Color = #111827 = [0.067, 0.094, 0.149] — 'Surface (cards) / Slightly lifted from background' from §3.1 Corporate Dark Stack. The center is subtly brighter than the edges, creating a soft vignette in reverse (center brightened slightly).

Ramp End Point at [960, 1200] — below frame bottom, creating a large soft radial gradient. The large radius means the gradient is extremely subtle (a few steps of lightness from center to edge), not a dramatic spotlight.

Ramp End Color = #0B0B0F — matching the absolute darkest background. Edge of the radial gradient is the full near-black, creating the subtle center-lift vignette. The gradient spans ~50 brightness steps, barely perceptible but adds dimensionality.

Logo placeholder text layer at 140pt ExtraBold. In production, this layer would be replaced with an imported logo file (use replace_layer_source or import_file). The placeholder uses Inter ExtraBold at 140pt — suitable proxy for a wordmark-style logo. Tracking +200 on all-caps per §3.3: 'All-caps titles (any size): +100 to +200 minimum.' #F9FAFB warm white for maximum contrast.

Logo centered at [960, 540] — exact frame center. Logo reveals are almost always centered for maximum impact and symmetrical composition. Per §3.2: 'Logo/watermark placement: Lower-right crash point is the natural resting position — UNLESS for a hero logo reveal, where center commands presence.'

Logo opacity: 0 → 100 over 0.8s. Per §1.2: 'Simple logo reveal: 18–36 frames @24fps = 750ms–1.5s.' At 29.97fps, 0.8s ≈ 24 frames — within the 18–36 frame range. The concurrent scale animation (next step) makes this feel like a materialize/crystallize effect rather than a plain fade.

Logo scale: 90% → 100% over 0.8s. Per brief: 'Logo starts at opacity 0, scale 90%. Entrance: opacity 0→100, scale 90→100% with easeOutCubic over 0.8s.' The 10% scale difference creates a sense of expansion — the logo 'grows in' as it materializes. This is more energetic than a pure fade and communicates confidence. Per §1.3: 'Scale from 0 to 100% (snappy): 6–10 frames @24fps = 250–417ms' — our 24-frame version is weighted/cinematic.

easeOut on logo opacity. easeOutCubic (0.215, 0.61, 0.355, 1) behavior — the cubic family is 'most used by professionals' per §1.1. Starts fast (logo appears quickly) and decelerates to full visibility.

easeOut on logo scale. The 90%→100% scale animation decelerates at the 100% landing — the logo expands quickly then eases into its final size. This sets up the inertial bounce expression perfectly, which takes over at t=0.8s and adds the overshoot-settle.

Inertial Bounce expression on Scale (Harry Frank / Gray Machine, §1.4). Parameters: amp=0.03, freq=3.0, decay=5.0 = 'Spring UI element: single clean bounce that settles in ~0.4s' from §1.4 preset table. The expression reads the velocity of the Scale keyframes at t=0.8 and uses it to drive a damped oscillation. When the scale hits 100% with forward velocity, it overshoots slightly (~3% based on amp), bounces back, and settles. This is the §1.6 overshoot-settle principle: 'Overshoot signals life. Every object that stops should slightly overshoot and settle.' Expression is ES3 compliant: no const/let, no arrow functions, no template literals.

Apply CC Light Sweep to the logo layer. Per §2.4: 'CC Light Sweep best use: instant logo sheen, light streaks, surface scanning effects. Location: Effect > Generate > CC Light Sweep.' This is the primary wow-factor effect in this template — the light sweep gives the impression of a physical surface being illuminated, like chrome or glass catching light.

CC Light Sweep Width = 120. Per §2.4: 'Width: 50–200 (narrow beam vs. wide glow).' 120 creates a medium-wide sweep band — wide enough to be clearly visible as a light event, narrow enough to maintain shape clarity. Wider (150–200) would look more cinematic; narrower (50–80) would look more precise/metallic.

Sweep Intensity = 80%. Per §2.4: 'Sweep Intensity: 50–100%.' 80% is strong enough to clearly read as a light event on screen but stops short of overexposing the logo. At 100%, lighter logos may blow out to white during the sweep.

Edge Intensity = 50%. Per §2.4: 'Edge Intensity: 30–80% (adds depth/bevel to alpha edges).' 50% creates subtle bevel highlights at the logo alpha edges, adding perceived 3D depth and premium material quality.

Shape = Sharp (value 0). Per §2.4: 'Shape: Sharp (metallic), Smooth (soft glow).' Sharp shape creates a defined, high-quality metallic sheen — appropriate for a corporate brand reveal. Smooth would create a softer, more cinematic feel.

CC Light Sweep Direction keyframes: 0° → 180° over 0.8s, starting at t=0.8 (exactly when logo finishes its entrance animation). Per §2.4 technique: 'At frame 0: set keyframe on Center (far left). At ~1 second: keyframe Center (far right).' Direction 0° = horizontal sweep, animating to 180° sweeps the light 180° across the logo surface. This creates a rotational light sweep that reads as premium and dynamic.

ease_in_out on the light sweep direction. The sweep should accelerate and decelerate naturally — not sweep at constant speed. ease_in_out creates the feel of a real light source passing over the surface: slower at the edges (entering/exiting), faster through the middle.

Apply ADBE Glow (native AE Glow effect: Effect > Stylize > Glow) to the logo layer. Glow is applied AFTER CC Light Sweep in the effect stack so the glow amplifies the sweep's light contribution. Per §1.3 Principle 7: 'After a logo builds, a subtle glow pulses once.' Here the glow is constant and ambient — providing sustained luminosity rather than a transient pulse.

Glow Threshold = 60. Controls which luminance values receive glow (0–100, lower = more of the layer glows). 60 applies glow to the brighter half of the logo tones — highlights and midtones glow, shadows stay clean. This preserves logo shape clarity while adding luminosity to bright areas.

Glow Radius = 30. Tight enough to hug the logo shape (not a large diffuse bloom), wide enough to create visible soft luminance falloff around edges. A radius of 30 at 1920px creates a glow that extends ~1.5% of frame width from logo edges — subtle and premium.

Glow Intensity = 0.6. At 0.6, the glow is clearly present but doesn't blow out the logo. Per the customizable_fields default, this can be reduced to 0.3 for premium restraint or increased to 1.0 for neon-adjacent look. The §1.3 guideline for secondary action applies: the glow should support the logo, not compete with it.

Glow Colors = Original Colors (value 0). Using the logo's own colors for the glow — not a fixed color. This keeps the glow brand-accurate regardless of the logo color. The glow appears as a soft luminous halo of the same hue as the logo edge it surrounds.

Glow Intensity keyframes: 0 during entrance (t=0–0.8), 0 during light sweep (t=0.8–1.6), then ramps to 0.6 after sweep completes (t=1.6). This timing sequence creates a three-beat logo reveal: 1) emerge (scale/opacity), 2) swept by light (CC Light Sweep), 3) settle with glow (ADBE Glow). Each effect has its moment without competing.
easeOut on glow intensity fade-in. The glow rises quickly then settles to its sustained level — a fast initial glow-on that decelerates to the ambient steady state.

Top-level color grade adjustment layer. Applies a subtle teal-shadow / warm-highlight grade over the entire composition — the cinematic orange-teal split from §3.4: 'Shadows: push toward teal (#0EA5E9). Highlights: push toward orange/amber (#F97316). This is the standard Hollywood blockbuster color grade.' At very low intensity, this adds subtle depth without making the logo look unnatural.

Apply Curves (ADBE Curves) to the grade adjustment layer. Curves is the professional standard for color correction — allows independent control of R/G/B channels and luminance. This will be used to subtly push shadows toward teal and highlights toward warm amber.

Color grade adjustment layer at 30% opacity — very subtle. This is a grading touch, not a dominant effect. At 30%, the teal-warm split adds a cinematic quality that the viewer perceives as polish without being able to identify as color grading. Lower than 20% would be imperceptible; higher than 50% would look processed.
